title: Quiz: Laser Cooling
description: Test your understanding of Doppler and sideband cooling techniques

questions.question: In Doppler cooling, why is the laser red-detuned from the atomic transition?

options.text: So atoms moving toward the laser see it Doppler-shifted into resonance and absorb more
To increase the spontaneous emission rate

questions.explanation: Red detuning means atoms moving toward the laser see the light blue-shifted into resonance, absorbing preferentially when moving toward the laser. This creates a velocity-dependent force that damps motion.

questions.question: What is the Doppler temperature limit for a transition with linewidth Gamma?
questions.explanation: The Doppler temperature limit T_D = hbar*Gamma/(2*k_B) arises from the balance between cooling (photon absorption) and heating (spontaneous emission recoil).

questions.question: In resolved sideband cooling, what transition is driven to remove phonons?
questions.explanation: The red sideband transition |g,n> -> |e,n-1> removes one phonon. After spontaneous decay back to |g,n-1>, the net effect is one less phonon.

questions.question: Resolved sideband cooling requires the trap frequency to be larger than the transition linewidth.
questions.explanation: True! For resolved sidebands, we need omega_trap >> Gamma so the red and blue sidebands are spectrally distinguishable from the carrier.

questions.question: What is sympathetic cooling?

options.text: Using one ion species to cool another through Coulomb interaction
Cooling by reducing the trap voltage
A slower version of Doppler cooling

questions.explanation: Sympathetic cooling uses 'coolant' ions that are laser-cooled to extract energy from 'logic' ions through their Coulomb interaction, without needing direct laser access to the logic ion's cooling transition.
